turns.content: I have a script that generates invalid code using a custom proc-macro, and I'm trying to verify it fails compilation. My current approach wraps the macro output in a String and passes it to trybuild as a file content string, but the tests keep passing even though the generated code shouldn't compile.

That sounds like a common gotcha when dealing with macro expansion testing. The issue is likely that you're passing the raw String output to trybuild instead of letting the compiler actually parse and expand it. You need to use the compile-fail feature on your test module so that the generated code goes through normal compilation, not just string parsing.

Right, I tried using #[test] with a doc comment containing the macro expansion, but trybuild just checks if the doc compiles without actually running my proc-macro. Is there a way to force it to expand?

You're on the right track about the compilation flow. The key is that your test module itself needs to be configured with compile_fail, and you should use the macro directly in the test code rather than just passing a string representation of it.
So I should define a test function where I actually call my proc-macro inline? Like using the generated type or trait impl in the test body itself?

Exactly. You want your test module to import and use the macro-generated types directly, then wrap that whole module with #[test] #[compile_fail]. This forces the compiler to run your proc-macro during compilation and catch any errors it produces.
What about using trybuild's expect-fail feature? Does that work better for macro expansion testing?

trybuild does support compile_fail assertions, but you still need the code to actually be compiled. The difference is that expect-fail will just skip the test if it passes compilation, which defeats your purpose of verifying macro expansion failures.
I'm getting confused about whether I should use serde_json::Value to represent my generated types or keep them as actual Rust types in my test module. Which approach is better for this scenario?
For macro expansion testing, you definitely want the actual Rust types in your test module. Using serde_json::Value would bypass the compiler entirely since it's just data serialization.
Got it. But what if my proc-macro generates something complex like a trait impl that I need to verify compiles correctly?

That's perfect for the compile_fail approach you're planning. Just make sure your test module imports the generated trait and implements it, then wrap everything with #[compile_fail]. The compiler will fail on the invalid parts of your macro output.
One last thing - does trybuild support custom error messages from my proc-macro? Or do I need to parse stderr manually?

trybuild captures the compiler output in the test failure message by default. You can access it via the TestFailure structure, but for basic macro expansion verification, you usually just want the compilation to fail naturally without needing custom parsing.
I'm trying to test a macro that's supposed to fail compilation, but I can't seem to get the expected error message. Should I just use a simple assert!() inside my tests instead?

Using assert!() won't work for verifying that your macro produces an actual compiler error because assert!() only runs at runtime when your code compiles successfully, whereas you need to catch errors that happen during the compilation phase itself. For this specific use case where you want to verify macro expansion behavior by expecting a compile-time failure, trybuild is designed exactly for this purpose since it can parse the output of your build process and match against expected error messages.
Okay, so I need to write a test that expects my Cargo.toml or test manifest to contain specific error text? How do I configure trybuild in my project setup?

You add trybuild as a dev-dependency in your Cargo.toml since it only needs access to the source code during compilation and doesn't need runtime binaries. Then you create a test file where you define a pass or fail attribute on each function, specifying exactly what error message you expect from the macro expansion by using the expected_err() helper method provided by trybuild's crate.

Wait, I thought macros only run at compile time so they can't be tested like regular functions? I assumed all macro logic had to be static and couldn't be verified through test harnesses. Also, what exactly does the pass attribute do since my macro always seems to fail in normal builds?

Macros are executed by the compiler during compilation but their output can vary based on input arguments or conditional logic, which is why you need a dynamic way to verify them rather than relying on static assumptions. The pass and fail attributes tell trybuild whether your test should succeed if no errors occur or succeed only if specific errors appear, so for a macro designed to trigger failures, you'd use the fail attribute with expected_err() to match the exact error string produced by your macro.

That makes sense about the failure mode, but I'm confused why my test isn't running at all. Is trybuild actually compiling my code or just checking the output? And if my macro uses proc-macro attributes that aren't being recognized, does that prevent the test from executing in the first place?

Trybuild compiles your entire crate including your macros and then captures stdout/stderr to compare against what you specified in your tests; it doesn't just check output files without compilation. Proc-macro attributes like #[proc_macro] or #[derive(Deserialize)] can affect whether the compiler recognizes your macro correctly, but trybuild still runs the normal build process so if your proc-macro implementation has a bug preventing expansion, that error will be captured and matched against your expected_err() string.

I think I misunderstood how macros work. I thought once a macro is defined it always produces the same output regardless of context, but clearly from what you're saying, my macro can behave differently depending on the test environment? Isn't that just because I'm using different arguments in each test case? Can I control this behavior through trybuild configuration?

You don't need to change trybuild's configuration since it always runs your code exactly as written; what varies is whether you're testing a macro that conditionally expands based on its inputs or a macro with side effects like logging. If your macro has conditional logic inside it, the pass/fail attributes in your tests determine which branch of behavior you want to verify by selecting specific error messages that only appear under certain conditions.

So I should just write multiple test functions each with different expected_err() strings? But what if my macro generates complex errors involving multiple lines or formatting issues? How does trybuild handle those cases where the output doesn't match exactly line for line?

Trybuild matches against partial error messages using regex patterns so you can specify a substring rather than requiring an exact full-line match, which handles cases where your macro produces complex multi-line errors or formatting variations. You can use regex::Regex to define the expected pattern in your test function arguments, allowing flexibility when dealing with dynamic formatting from macros that might include timestamps, file paths, or variable data in their error output.

That sounds helpful for the flexible matching, but I'm still worried about false positives. What if my macro accidentally produces an error message similar to what I expect by coincidence? Is there a way to make trybuild more strict or verify that the actual compilation failure stems from my specific macro logic rather than unrelated code in my crate?

You can group your tests under a specific module path and use the ignore attribute on unrelated test functions to exclude them, ensuring only the relevant tests run. For stricter verification, you could combine trybuild with custom assertions that check whether the error message contains keywords related to your macro's expected failure condition, like checking if 'name' appears in the error string rather than just matching arbitrary text.

I see, so I should probably add some additional validation logic on top of what trybuild provides? But wait, isn't there a trait that lets me customize how errors are reported without writing extra code? Can I use something like Result::unwrap() or Option::expect() to simplify this further instead of relying solely on regex patterns?

You don't need custom validation logic beyond what trybuild provides since it already handles the core matching; however, you can combine it with assert!() checks for runtime behavior if needed. The trait you're thinking of is likely from a different crate that might offer additional error handling features, but for macro testing specifically, trybuild's expected_err() combined with regex patterns covers most cases without requiring extra code beyond defining your test functions.

Okay, I think I understand the flow now. But just to be sure, what happens if my macro actually expands successfully instead of failing? Does trybuild still run the test or does it skip entirely since there's no error to match against? And how do I distinguish between a macro that silently succeeds versus one that should fail but produces warnings instead of errors?

If your macro expands successfully without producing any errors, then your test will fail because you specified expected_err() expecting failures. Trybuild runs all tests regardless of whether the code compiles or not, so it will report a failure if no error was produced when one was expected. For distinguishing between warnings and errors, you can use trybuild's ignore attribute on specific tests to suppress certain output categories, or use separate test functions with different pass/fail attributes to verify that your macro produces only errors in some cases but warnings in others.

Thanks for clarifying the distinction between warnings and errors. But one last thing - what if I want to test both success and failure scenarios in a single test file? Is there a way to conditionally run different tests based on whether my macro produces an error or not, rather than having separate functions for each case?

You can't dynamically control which tests run based on runtime behavior since trybuild only executes during the compilation phase and doesn't have access to your code's execution state. Instead, you should write separate test functions where each explicitly specifies its expected outcome through pass or fail attributes with appropriate error messages, ensuring that every possible macro behavior is covered by a dedicated test case in your test file.

I have a working solution using trybuild to test my macro expansion, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle the specific case where the macro generates invalid syntax that should cause compilation errors.

Using trybuild is actually quite standard for this job since it wraps your Rust compiler invocation and parses the output. Think of it like a specialized scanner that reads your code, runs the build command, and then checks if the error log matches what you expect rather than just looking at raw text. You can define assertions in your test file to specify exactly which errors should appear when certain macros are invoked with bad arguments.

That analogy helps visualize it as a filter but I need more detail on how trybuild handles proc-macro output specifically. When my macro expands into something that fails compilation, does trybuild automatically capture the error message or do I have to manually parse stderr?

trybuild doesn't just grab the raw stderr; it provides structured assertions so you can check specific properties of the failure without manual parsing. For example, you might assert that a particular macro invocation produces an error mentioning 'unexpected token' instead of checking if the string contains those words in some random order. This makes testing more robust because it validates the semantic meaning of the error rather than just pattern matching text.

Okay so I shouldn't rely on string matching for this scenario, but what happens when my macro uses conditional compilation with cfg attributes and I want to test different code paths? Does trybuild handle that complexity or do I need a custom harness?

trybuild can work with cfg conditions but it becomes tricky if your macros depend on them since the expansion changes based on flags. You might find it easier to use a testing framework like cargo-expand alongside trybuild to manually inspect how your macro expands under different configurations before asserting that the resulting code is syntactically invalid.

Cargo-expand seems promising for debugging but I'm concerned about performance overhead during CI runs. Is there a way to run these tests without triggering full compilation or just checking the expansion output?

You can configure trybuild to skip actual compilation in some scenarios by focusing on the assertion logic that validates macro expansion results without needing the binary to be built. However, for true integration testing where you need to verify that invalid macro output actually breaks compilation, running a full compile is often necessary because the compiler itself enforces syntax rules that static analysis tools like cargo-expand might miss.

I see, so the trade-off is between performance and accuracy when validating proc-macro outputs. But what if I want to test both successful macro invocations and failures in the same suite? Does trybuild support mixing these test cases effectively?

Yes, trybuild allows you to mix passing and failing tests within the same test file by using different assertion blocks for each scenario. You can group your tests into suites where one suite verifies that valid macro inputs produce correct code while another suite ensures invalid inputs generate expected errors.
That sounds efficient but how do I structure my test file to organize these scenarios clearly? Should I use separate modules or just different functions in the same file?

You can keep everything in a single test file using different #[test] functions for each scenario, which keeps your code organized without needing multiple files. Just ensure you name them descriptively so it's clear which tests validate successful expansions and which ones verify compilation failures.

I'm trying to write tests for a macro I built, but the documentation feels too abstract. When I run my test suite with trybuild, it seems like it just checks if the code compiles without errors, which isn't enough.

You're right that basic compilation checks aren't sufficient for macro logic verification. The issue is that trybuild needs to understand how your macro expands into actual Rust code before it can verify the output. You need to set up a test configuration where trybuild captures the expanded macro tree and compares it against expected results.

So I thought I could just write assert_eq! on the source code string itself, but that won't work because macros don't produce strings directly, they produce concrete syntax trees. I need to use trybuild's expand feature instead.

Exactly. You cannot test macro output by comparing raw source code since macros transform that into something else entirely. Instead, you must configure your test harness to invoke the compiler with specific flags like -C debug-assertions or use trybuild's expand mode which captures the actual expanded syntax tree and verifies it matches expectations.

Wait, I misunderstood something earlier. I thought I could just pass a macro to my test function and expect it to fail compilation if wrong, but that's not how trybuild works because it doesn't run the actual binary.

You got that right on the critical point. Trybuild doesn't execute your code or build a running binary; instead, it runs the compiler in verification mode to check if your macro expansion produces valid Rust syntax and matches expected patterns. You configure trybuild to capture intermediate compilation steps rather than waiting for final error messages.
Okay so I should be using proc-macro crates to generate test cases dynamically instead of manually writing them? That sounds like what I was thinking but got the direction wrong.

You're partially correct about dynamic generation, but not in the way you thought. Instead of using proc-macro crates to create test cases, you should use trybuild's built-in configuration options like cfg-test or specific flag combinations that make it capture macro expansion output directly without needing external code generators.

Ah I see now. So the key insight is that trybuild captures the actual expanded macro tree at compile time, not just error messages? That means I can compare specific token sequences rather than whole file contents?

Precisely. By using trybuild's expand mode, you capture the exact syntax tree that results from macro expansion at compile time. This lets you verify individual tokens or structural patterns within the expanded output rather than comparing entire file contents or waiting for runtime errors.

That makes sense now. I was confusing trybuild with cargo-expand which outputs to stdout, but trybuild actually integrates directly into the test harness and returns structured results instead of printing to console.

You nailed it. Trybuild's integration into your test framework means you get structured verification data rather than raw stdout output from cargo-expand. This allows you to programmatically assert on specific aspects of macro expansion without needing separate compilation runs or external tools.
So my final understanding is that I configure trybuild to capture expanded syntax trees, then use those trees in assertions instead of comparing source code strings directly?

Yes. That's exactly the pattern you should follow: configure trybuild to capture macro expansion output as syntax trees, then perform assertions on those expanded structures rather than trying to match raw source code patterns.

I'm debugging a production issue where my custom macro keeps generating invalid code in tests. I've been trying to test the output of this proc-macro by writing assertions on the expanded string, but the tests keep failing even when the macro looks correct. Is there a better way to verify what a proc-macro actually produces without relying on manual string comparisons?

You are likely running into limitations with basic assertion macros because they don't understand Rust's internal representation of code. When you test raw strings from macro expansion, you miss the semantic meaning of the generated tokens. The idiomatic approach for this situation is to use trybuild instead of manual string matching. trybuild allows you to define expected compilation errors directly in your tests by specifying error messages that the compiler should produce when running with specific flags.

That sounds promising, but I've tried using trybuild and my tests still fail unexpectedly. It seems like the macro output isn't being captured correctly for the assertion. How do I configure a test to expect a specific compilation failure from this proc-macro?

When testing macros with trybuild, you need to ensure your test harness is configured to expand the macro before compiling it. You should define a helper function in your test module that returns a string literal representing the input code and pass that through the macro invocation during compilation. Then use assert_errs from the trybuild crate to compare the actual compiler output against expected errors defined as strings or regex patterns.

I understand I need to configure the test harness, but what exactly does this look like in a real test case? Can you show me how to set up a test that expects the macro to generate code which then fails compilation with a specific error message?

In your test file, you would declare a const string literal holding the input code and use it as an argument to the proc-macro. The trybuild crate will capture stderr from the compiler and compare it against expected messages. You might also need to enable certain features in Cargo.toml like testing with specific rustflags or using the --test flag when running tests.

Okay, so I should be passing a string literal into the macro? But wait, isn't that exactly what I was doing before and why is it still not working? Is there something else about how trybuild works that I'm missing?

You are probably confusing manual string assertions with actual compiler error capturing. When you manually assert on a string output from the macro, you are testing the macro's logic rather than its integration with the compiler. With trybuild, you aren't asserting on strings directly; instead, you are asserting on the compiler's behavior when running your code. The key difference is that trybuild captures the actual compilation errors produced by the Rust compiler, not just what the macro prints to stdout.

Ah, so I shouldn't be testing the string output of the proc-macro at all? But my goal is specifically to verify that this macro generates code that will fail in a specific way. How do I tell trybuild to expect a compilation error from a macro-generated block?

You should structure your test so that the macro generates code inside a module or function, and then assert_errs checks if compiling that entire unit fails with an expected message. Instead of testing the macro's output directly, you test the full compilation unit containing the macro expansion. You can use helper functions in your test suite to prepare inputs for trybuild assertions.

I think I get it now. So instead of manually parsing the macro output, I should write a test that includes the macro usage and expects the compiler to fail with a specific error. But what if my macro sometimes generates valid code? Do I need multiple tests?

Yes, you would create separate test cases for different scenarios. One test case using trybuild asserts on expected compilation failures when the macro produces invalid syntax or logic errors. Another test case might use assert_no_failures to verify that under certain conditions the macro generates valid code that compiles successfully. This dual approach ensures your tests cover both failure and success paths.

That makes sense for covering different scenarios, but I'm still confused about how trybuild actually captures those errors. Is there a specific trait or mechanism that allows it to intercept compiler output during the test run?

trybuild implements the TestHarness trait internally and integrates with cargo's test runner by capturing stderr from the rustc compiler process when compiling your test code. It parses this output and compares it against expected error messages you define in your test functions using assert_errs or similar methods provided by the crate.
Okay, so I just need to write my tests with assert_errs and let trybuild handle the rest? Is there any configuration in Cargo.toml required besides the dependencies?

You should add trybuild to your dev-dependencies section in Cargo.toml along with other testing crates like serde or similar. You might also need to adjust compiler flags if you are using nightly features, but standard configurations usually work out of the box.
Wait, one last thing - what about macros that generate code at compile time? Does trybuild actually expand them before checking for errors?

Yes, trybuild expands all proc-macros in your test file before compiling and comparing compiler output. This ensures you are testing the actual expanded code rather than just seeing macro invocation strings. You can verify this by adding debug prints or logging to see what's happening during compilation.
